topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. Sarah, my supervisor, leaned across the desk, her smile wide and entirely too saccharine. "So, Mark is taking over next week," she chirped, tapping a pen against the file. "I thought you could give him a thorough orientation. You know the system inside and out." A tight knot formed in my stomach, tightening with every syllable. I felt my jaw clench so hard I could taste copper. "Thorough?" I managed, my voice coming out sharper than intended. "I’ve been doing this job for three years. I don't have time for a handover session; I have actual work to do." I pushed back from my chair, the scrape of the metal legs against the linoleum sounding deafening in the quiet corner.

[story 2]

I stared at the whiteboard, the hastily drawn flowcharts suddenly seeming mocking in their simplicity. When David called me in, his tone was overly cheerful, almost patronizing. "Hey, Alex. Just wanted to chat about the transition to Chloe. She’s really eager to learn, so I thought you could be her guide." My knuckles were white where I gripped my coffee mug. I took a slow, deliberate sip, letting the heat scald my tongue. "Guide?" I echoed, my voice dangerously level. "I’m not a babysitter. This isn't a casual chat; this is a complete knowledge transfer. I’ve already flagged three critical bottlenecks that need addressing before anyone touches the main server. Why do I have to spend my entire afternoon spoon-feeding someone who hasn't even been briefed on the basic protocols?" I stood up abruptly, the chair sliding back with a loud squeak.

[story 3]

The air in the training room felt thick, heavy with unspoken expectations. Mr. Henderson had set the scene with a practiced, gentle air, describing the new hire’s potential. I could feel the heat rising up my neck, a flush spreading across my cheeks that I desperately tried to mask with a neutral expression. When he finally laid out the expectations—a full day dedicated to walking someone through every single procedure—a low, simmering anger began to bubble beneath my skin. "Sir," I began, my tone clipped and precise, every word measured and sharp, "with all due respect, my current workload is already at maximum capacity. I simply don't have the bandwidth to dedicate an entire day to onboarding someone new. If this is mandatory, perhaps we can streamline the process, or assign someone else who has the capacity." I watched his composure waver for the briefest moment before he regained control, a flicker of annoyance crossing his face.
